ontology.foodex2_description: Mustard leaves and similar (Brassica juncea), cooked
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for boiled Indian mustard cabbage; SNOMED CT coverage for individual cooked vegetables is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of boiled kai choy is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl of cooked leafy greens served alongside rice or a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Boiled Indian Mustard Cabbage (Kai Choy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for boiled Indian mustard cabbage (Kai choy), CR ID F14010010. All core macros and micronutrients are as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated as negligible (<0.1 g/100 g) based on USDA FoodData Central entry for boiled mustard greens (FDC ID 169256), documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — non-starchy leafy vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled Indian mustard cabbage (kai choy) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on the closest analogous non-starchy leafy green vegetables (e.g., boiled cabbage, boiled spinach) which consistently fall in the GI range of 10–20 in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (15 × 0.7 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.105, rounded to 0.1. This is a very low GL consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (469 mg/100 g) with calcium (135 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled kai choy is highly suitable for hypertension management; its very low sodium content and high potassium-to-sodium ratio strongly support blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary principles, provided no high-sodium sauces are added during or after cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.7 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~15), and meaningful dietary fibre (1.9 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled kai choy is an excellent choice for individuals with type 2 diabetes; its negligible carbohydrate load, low estimated glycemic index, and fibre content contribute to minimal postprandial blood glucose elevation and support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, high potassium, high calcium, and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled kai choy supports cardiovascular health through its negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (469 mg/100 g) for vasodilation, and dietary fibre (1.9 g/100 g) that contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction; it is appropriate for regular inclusion in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory cruciferous leafy green with high calcium (135 mg/100 g) and antioxidant phytonutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled kai choy is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; its calcium content supports bone protection in those on corticosteroid therapy, and its cruciferous antioxidants provide anti-inflammatory benefit, but high-sodium condiments should be avoided to prevent worsening of tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled Indian mustard cabbage, commonly known as kai choy or gai choy (Brassica juncea), is a leafy green vegetable widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and broader Southeast Asia. The vegetable is prepared by boiling fresh mustard cabbage leaves until tender, resulting in a soft texture with a mild peppery and slightly bitter flavour characteristic of the mustard family. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice, congee, or as part of a mixed vegetable plate in hawker centres and home cooking. Nutritionally, boiled kai choy is a low-calorie food at only 19 kcal per 100 g, with a notable protein content of 2.4 g/100 g for a leafy vegetable, very low carbohydrate (0.7 g/100 g), and minimal fat (0.3 g/100 g). It is exceptionally rich in potassium (469 mg/100 g) and calcium (135 mg/100 g), making it clinically relevant for hypertension management and bone health. Sodium is negligible at 3 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre at 1.9 g/100 g supports digestive health and glycaemic control. The estimated glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, it is appropriate for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management when prepared without high-sodium sauces.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F14010010) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central boiled mustard greens (FDC ID 169256) as HPB does not report these fields. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy leafy green category. Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.06 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.14 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for boiled mustard greens (FDC ID 169256, SR Legacy) as HPB Singapore does not report fatty acid breakdown for this item. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for this food type. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific entry (F14010010), and cross-referencing with USDA FoodData Central for boiled mustard greens provides estimates but these are not directly attributed to the HPB-sourced record to preserve source integrity. 3. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100 g) per HPB source — this is naturally zero for a plant-based leafy vegetable. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy green vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for boiled kai choy is available. 5. The input record listed vitamin C as approximately 50 mg/100 g in the health_suitability narrative; this figure is not confirmed by the HPB per_100g data and has not been included in per_100g to maintain source integrity. 6. Keto dietary tag retained from input as carbohydrate content (0.7 g/100 g) is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds.
